meeting_purpose_blurb: Council examines particular topics in depth. No official decisions are made and no public comment is taken.
lead_headline: Arvada Council Examines Wildfire Plans and Upcoming Utility Rate Process

type_specific.meeting_summary: Arvada City Council held two workshops on May 12, 2026. In the first, emergency management staff and Arvada Fire presented wildfire mitigation and response strategies, covering preparedness, the Community Wildfire Protection Plan, a new wildfire resiliency code, public safety power shutoffs, prescribed fire, and the Jefferson County hazard mitigation plan update. In the second, utility staff provided a foundational briefing on the 2027 water, wastewater, and stormwater rates and fees process, including infrastructure planning, affordability programs, and a proposed community advisory group.
type_specific.lead_headline: Arvada Council Examines Wildfire Plans and Upcoming Utility Rate Process
type_specific.meeting_purpose_blurb: Council examines particular topics in depth. No official decisions are made and no public comment is taken.

workshop_topics.title: Wildfire Mitigation and Response Strategies
workshop_topics.tagline: City and fire officials outline preparedness, evacuation gaps, and community outreach efforts
workshop_topics.lede: Emergency management staff (identified in transcript context as Brian) and Arvada Fire's Steven Parker jointly presented the city's current wildfire preparedness framework. They covered the recently completed Community Wildfire Protection Plan, a newly adopted wildfire resiliency code, fire department response levels, public safety power shutoffs (PSPS), prescribed fire, neighborhood mitigation projects, and the ongoing Jefferson County hazard mitigation plan update. No formal decisions were requested; the session was intended to brief council and gather questions ahead of future policy work.

options.label: Add evacuation analysis requirements to development review
options.summary: Staff noted the city currently lacks formal policy tools to require evacuation analysis in development proposals, and pointed to Colorado Springs and Boulder County ordinances as models. Staff expressed support for developing a similar mechanism for Arvada.

options.label: Expand Lookout Alert community signups
options.summary: Continue and intensify ongoing efforts to increase resident enrollment in the Lookout Alert emergency notification system, including outreach through the Arvada Chamber and neighborhood events.

options.label: Develop pre-event public information on PSPS charging/warming stations
options.summary: Publish in advance the locations of charging stations, warming centers, and go-bag guidance so residents can plan before a public safety power shutoff occurs, coordinated with Jefferson County.

options.label: Pursue prescribed fire and mechanical fuels mitigation projects
options.summary: Continue and expand fuel reduction efforts including mechanical clearing (limbing, debris removal), grazing contracts, mowing, and prescribed burns on open space, guided by CWPP priorities and subject to CDPHE and state approvals.

options.label: Establish homeowner wildfire grant program through Arvada Fire
options.cost: Small amount budgeted for 2026 (exact figure not stated)
options.summary: Arvada Fire is developing a small grant program for residents to apply for financial assistance with home hardening and defensible space improvements on their own property.

member_positions.name: Bob Fifer
member_positions.option_preference: Engage Jefferson County mitigation crews for unincorporated area fuel reduction

quote.speaker: Bob Fifer
quote.text: I was talking to Brian over there keing Keing about those mitigation crews. So if they're not fighting fires, can they get that whole crew just rolling? Um, obviously they got to hire 45 people or whatever, but um, I just curious how much we're engaged with them to address that since some of it's out of the city.
quote.context_excerpt: I was talking to Brian over there keing Keing about those mitigation crews. So if they're not fighting fires, can they get that whole crew just rolling? Um, obviously they got to hire 45 people or whatever, but um, I just curious how much we're engaged with them to address that since some of it's out of the city. Um, as just a side note, just so you know, and I think you know this, you know, Highway 72 to Highway 119 is the highest burn probability in the state.

member_positions.name: Randy Moorman
member_positions.option_preference: Consolidate public-facing wildfire resources and promote home assessments

quote.speaker: Randy Moorman
quote.text: where where should we direct them to to get easy accessible resources on what to do and how to prepare?
quote.context_excerpt: I'm trying to keep track of the different pieces like the community wildfire protection plan, the wildfire resiliency code, the WOOI code and then you talked about the Jeffco mitigation plan. So, um, there's a lot of moving parts. And so, I guess my my question for you is for our general public, where where should we direct them to to get easy accessible resources on what to do and how to prepare?

member_positions.name: Randy Moorman
member_positions.option_preference: Integrate renewable/solar energy into emergency response for PSPS resilience

quote.speaker: Randy Moorman
quote.text: I'm I'm going to sound like a broken record council but I think you know having a more sustainable local source of energy uh to provide our residents in those times of the shut off whether it's a micro grid system, you know, solar gardens that we can tap into in battery system. There's many things we can be looking at to help have a more robust, resilient community when we do have those power shut offs.
quote.context_excerpt: my my next question is um with our emergency office um you know we're going to be looking at our um climate action sustainability plan next week and so I hope you're in coordination with that because you know especially comes to mind the public safety power shut offs and you know I'm I'm going to sound like a broken record council but I think you know having a more sustainable local source of energy uh to provide our residents in those times of the shut off whether it's a micro grid system, you know, solar gardens that we can tap into in battery system.

member_positions.name: Lauren Simpson
member_positions.option_preference: Develop and socialize pre-event PSPS public information (Option 3)

quote.speaker: Lauren Simpson
quote.text: I I guess that's sort of my request is can we have those messages available? Can we start starting to socialize that information now with the public?
quote.context_excerpt: I'm also thinking about what we can do in the immediate term to help our residents. Um, and this, I guess, maybe is a sort of a request for support. Are there information that we can be sharing out now about, you know, what to have in your go bag? I know we have that, but where will charging stations be set up? Where can the public just know in advance before uh an event happens? ... I I guess that's sort of my request is can we have those messages available? Can we start starting to socialize that information now with the public?

workshop_topics.feeds_into: Jefferson County Hazard Mitigation Plan annex expected to return to council for adoption in fall 2026; evacuation policy mechanisms and development review changes flagged for future policy discussion at an unspecified future business meeting.

workshop_topics.title: 2027 Water, Wastewater, and Stormwater Rates and Fees — Kickoff Workshop
workshop_topics.tagline: Staff launches annual utility rate review with foundational briefing on infrastructure needs and affordability programs
workshop_topics.lede: Utility Director Chris (last name not stated in transcript) and Finance staff member Jacqueline Gray presented the first in a series of annual workshops leading to council adoption of 2027 utility rates and fees. The session covered how enterprise funds work, the difference between rates/fees and system development charges, the city's 10-year financial planning framework, recent bond issuances, affordability programs, and a proposed community advisory group. No rate scenarios or cost figures were presented at this kickoff session; those are scheduled for the July 28 workshop.

options.label: Establish a community utilities advisory group (ad hoc, through 2030)
options.summary: Staff proposed standing up an ad hoc community advisory group — not a formal board — to provide public input on utility infrastructure trade-offs and financial strategies through approximately 2030, aligned with the current major reinvestment period.

options.label: Refine project criticality color-coding in 10-year capital plan
options.summary: Council member Fifer asked staff to ensure red-coded projects are genuinely critical and not simply elevated because other reds were completed, and to clearly separate distinct pipeline segments rather than bundling them.

options.label: Explore drought-based tiered pricing and family-size equity in water rates
options.summary: Council members raised the idea of reviewing tier structures to account for household size and to consider drought surcharges in higher tiers, with staff indicating drought pricing discussions are planned for coming months.

options.label: Align water conservation policy with tree canopy and urban agriculture goals
options.summary: Council member Griffith asked staff to work with the forestry department to ensure water conservation messaging and rate structures do not inadvertently discourage tree care or urban agriculture, and to research how other communities handle rural-versus-urban water use differences.

options.label: Add 'green' completed-projects tracking to capital plan presentation
options.summary: Mayor Simpson requested that future capital plan presentations include a separate section highlighting projects already delivered, so residents and council can see the tangible results of prior bond and rate investments.

member_positions.name: Shawna Ambrose
member_positions.option_preference: Continue reporting on payment compliance, liens, and condemnations related to shutoffs

quote.speaker: Shawna Ambrose
quote.text: I also am always concerned about how many condemnations have happened just due to the inability to pay for water. Usually it seems like since we've implemented the program um the condemnations that have happened it the water is not the only thing going on at that property. There's lots of other issues. Um so I think just making sure that we are as sensitive as possible to condemning a home just because inability to pay a water um continuing to be um paying attention to that. I would just like to hear report backs on it.
quote.context_excerpt: I also am always concerned about how many condemnations have happened just due to the inability to pay for water. Usually it seems like since we've implemented the program um the condemnations that have happened it the water is not the only thing going on at that property. There's lots of other issues. Um so I think just making sure that we are as sensitive as possible to condemning a home just because inability to pay a water um continuing to be um paying attention to that. I would just like to hear report backs on it.

member_positions.name: Randy Moorman
member_positions.option_preference: Support community advisory group (Option 1) and promote autopay enrollment

quote.speaker: Randy Moorman
quote.text: I first just want to commend you on the um the community advisory group. I think that's a fantastic idea and I'm I'm excited to see that get going.
quote.context_excerpt: Thank you. So yeah, I think both of us are asking the same question. So Mayor Pro Tim Mormon, uh thank you. I I first just want to commend you on the um the community advisory group. I think that's a fantastic idea and I'm I'm excited to see that get going. Um happy to help get the word out. So, is there on our website now a place to apply?

member_positions.name: Bob Fifer
member_positions.option_preference: Refine project criticality color-coding (Option 2) and explore tiered/drought pricing

quote.speaker: Bob Fifer
quote.text: I would like to see the red not just be the next critical item from going up. Red should be truly critical, high critical, not just go from orange to red just because all the reds are getting addressed.
quote.context_excerpt: Only thing I have is on the uh prioritization spreadsheet. It's on the one I probably brings that up all the time. Can we just make sure because I I felt like last time we were just moving things into criticality levels. I would like to see the red not just be the next critical item from going up. Red should be truly critical, high critical, not just go from orange to red just because all the reds are getting addressed. Does that make sense?

member_positions.name: Rebecka Lovisone
member_positions.option_preference: Pause on new advisory groups; explore existing boards before adding new ones (caution on Option 1)

quote.speaker: Rebecka Lovisone
quote.text: I would just also like to encourage us to pause whenever possible before creating additional boards and commissions or committees until we create clearer structural alignment for the groups we already have.
quote.context_excerpt: I appreciate the idea of strengthening community engagement around utilities. Uh, that said, I would just also like to encourage us to pause whenever possible before creating additional boards and commissions or committees until we create clearer structural alignment for the groups we already have. Uh so just a thought moving forward before adding something new. I think we should first ask is there an existing combination of boards and commissions that could advise the work who already exists.

member_positions.name: Shawna Ambrose
member_positions.option_preference: Prioritize nonfunctional turf reduction as core drought water conservation strategy (Option 4)

quote.speaker: Shawna Ambrose
quote.text: 60% of water use in the summertime is for residential lawns. So really that should be the priority and I think is the priority of smart drought policy is nonfunctional turf. Don't water that.
quote.context_excerpt: the other thing is that 60% of water use in the summertime is for residential lawns. So really that should be the priority and I think is the priority of smart drought policy is nonfunctional turf. Don't water that. You know if you need the water for your kids to play I mean the lawn for your kids to play in or it's a soccer field or it's you know um pets or something like that. Okay. but nonfunctional turf like we really have to accept we live in a desert.

member_positions.name: Lauren Simpson
member_positions.option_preference: Add completed-projects 'green' tracking to capital presentations (Option 5)

quote.speaker: Lauren Simpson
quote.text: We always want to celebrate the greens, the things that we have delivered on to maybe it can be on a separate page, but just to show the public, you know, it's not all red and orange and yellow that, you know, we have had these bonds working for the last several years. Here are the things we've delivered and we can and it makes us feel good when we start to see the stackup of all the things that we've delivered for our residents.
quote.context_excerpt: let's go back to our colors. Red, yellow, red, orange, and yellow. There's no green you guys. We always want to celebrate the greens, the things that we have delivered on to maybe it can be on a separate page, but just to show the public, you know, it's not all red and orange and yellow that, you know, we have had these bonds working for the last several years. Here are the things we've delivered and we can and it makes us feel good when we start to see the stackup of all the things that we've delivered for our residents.

workshop_topics.feeds_into: July 28, 2026 utility workshop (rate/fee scenarios by spending package); August 25, 2026 follow-up workshop; first and second reading for rate adoption in October 2026 alongside the budget process.

meeting_summary: Arvada City Council held two workshops on May 12, 2026. In the first, emergency management staff and Arvada Fire presented wildfire mitigation and response strategies, covering preparedness, the Community Wildfire Protection Plan, a new wildfire resiliency code, public safety power shutoffs, prescribed fire, and the Jefferson County hazard mitigation plan update. In the second, utility staff provided a foundational briefing on the 2027 water, wastewater, and stormwater rates and fees process, including infrastructure planning, affordability programs, and a proposed community advisory group.

alerts.title: Free Drop-Off Site Open at Long Lake
alerts.body: The City of Arvada is hosting a free tree limb drop-off starting Saturday, May 9 at Long Lake Regional Park.
